data.title: VICI Properties: Doubling Down On This 6%+ Yield Right Now
data.description:
My audience is overwhelmingly focused on dividend growth stocks. Given my pseudonym of Kody's Dividends, that's hardly a surprise.

As such, it's also probably not a stretch to assert that most are familiar with the term "yield trap." This is used to characterize a stock with an exceptionally high dividend yield. Oftentimes, this is a high single-digit percentage yield for C-Corps or a double-digit percentage yield for REITs/midstream companies.

Avoiding yield traps is arguably one of the most important aspects of being a dividend growth investor. That brings to mind the following quote of famed financial writer and market strategist Raymond DeVoe Jr.:

More money has been lost reaching for yield than at the point of a gun.

This is backed up by the hard data, too. Data from Hartford Funds shows that dividend cutters and eliminators have returned -0.89% annually from 1973 through 2024. This isn't even taking into account the loads of cumulative inflation that have materialized over the past five decades and counting.

Today, I'm going to be revisiting VICI Properties (VICI). Despite its elevated dividend yield, it's anything but a yield trap in my opinion. When I last covered VICI with a buy rating in January, I thought the company could keep growing AFFO per share at a steady clip. The REIT's net leverage ratio also seemed to be viable. Finally, shares looked to be moderately undervalued.

Eleven months later, I'm reaffirming my Buy rating. VICI's growth profile looks to be holding up. The company's balance sheet remains investment-grade. VICI's shares have also become a more enticing value.

VICI Properties Is Humming Along

On October 30th, VICI released its financial results for the third quarter ended September 30th, 2025. The company's total revenue grew by 4.4% year-over-year to $1.01 billion during the quarter. For more perspective, this topped Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter by $5.1 million. VICI's share count only ticked up by 1.9% over the year-ago period, which implies modest per-share revenue growth as well.

What was behind the company's top-line growth for the third quarter? The majority of VICI's total revenue growth came from a surge in income from loans and securities. This revenue source jumped 68.2% year-over-year to $61 million during the quarter. That was fueled by the origination and funding of debt investments and the interest income stemming from higher principal balances outstanding.

The other notable variable behind VICI's revenue growth in the third quarter came from higher leasing revenue. Leasing revenue increased by 1.9% over the year-ago period to $918.8 million for the quarter. Capital investments into the Venetian Resort and annual rent escalators from its other lease agreements were responsible for the growth in leasing revenue during the quarter.

VICI's AFFO per share rose by 5.3% year-over-year to $0.60 in the third quarter. Disciplined cost management helped its AFFO margin expand by about 170 basis points to 63.3% for the quarter. That's how AFFO per share growth surpassed revenue growth during the quarter.

Moving forward, VICI has ways that it can drive more AFFO per share growth for shareholders. For one, the company's minimum, contractual annual rent escalations provide 1.7% same-store rent growth. On its own, that's already almost 6x the 0.3% rate of select triple net lease REITs.

VICI has room for more rent growth upside, too. In 2025, 42% of VICI's rent roll was tied to CPI-linked escalators. Over the long term, this is set to gradually rise to around 90% by 2035.

Its modest payout ratio allows it to retain AFFO for reinvestment in other projects as well. That's another growth driver for the REIT.

What's more, VICI recently announced a $1.16 billion acquisition of seven Nevada-based casino properties from Golden Entertainment in a sale-leaseback deal. This will add $87 million of initial annual cash rent (with an initial lease term of 30 years and four five-year renewal options), which works out to a 7.5% cap rate.

Beginning in the third year of the lease, annual rent escalation will be 2%. As of Q2 2025, the property-level rent coverage ratio of 1.9x points to strong unit economics. VICI is also included in a right of first offer on future developments like Colorado Belle, which could position the partnership for growth down the line. The deal is expected to close in mid-2026.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for AFFO per share to grow by 3.4% in 2026 to $2.45 (following a 4.9% consensus growth rate to $2.37 in 2025). For 2027, another 2.9% increase in AFFO per share to $2.52 is being projected.

VICI is also financially sound. As of September 30th, 2025, the company's net leverage ratio was 5.0x. This is on the low end of its targeted range of between 5.0x and 5.5x. The company's debt profile is attractive as well, with 99% of its debt at fixed rates and 83% unsecured. Maturities in the coming years are manageable, ranging from as little as $750 million in unsecured bonds in 2031 to as much as $2 billion in unsecured bonds in 2028 and 2030, respectively. Its $3 billion revolving credit facility isn't set to expire until 2032. As a result, VICI possesses a BBB- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to VICI's Q3 2025 Earnings Press Release, VICI's Q3 2025 10-Q Filing, and VICI's November 2025 Investor Presentation).

Fair Value Is Now Around $35 A Share

In the last 11 months, VICI's shares have been essentially flat, posting a -1% total return in that time. Concerns over Las Vegas visitation and the underperformance of REITs in general were contributing factors to these stagnant returns.

The big picture for me is unchanged, however. VICI's shares offer a compelling risk-reward dynamic in my view. The forward 12-month P/AFFO ratio of 11.4 is a downright bargain. That's an entire turn cheaper than the previous forward 12-month P/AFFO ratio of 12.5 when I last covered the stock.

VICI's current valuation remains well below the seven-year average P/AFFO ratio of 15.7, according to FAST Graphs. That was down from the previous value of 16.2 because data from the lower valuation year of 2024 has since been included in the data set.

I believe that VICI can command a fair value P/AFFO ratio of 14.1. This would be one standard deviation less than the seven-year average P/AFFO ratio.

My rationale is that VICI's growth prospects are arguably holding up. I'm confident that the REIT can consistently grow AFFO per share by 3% to 4% annually.

The headwind to VICI's valuation remains the forward-looking interest rate atmosphere. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) is likely to remain elevated in the high-3% to low-4% range for the foreseeable future. For context, that would be materially higher than the seven-year average of 2.6%, per Macrotrends. This is how I arrive at my fair value P/AFFO ratio of 14.1.

As of today, the calendar year 2025 is 94% complete. That leaves another 6% of 2025 and 94% of 2026 yet to come over the next 12 months. This is how I arrive at a forward 12-month AFFO per share input of $2.45.

Using my fair value P/AFFO ratio of 14.1, I get a fair value of $35 a share. Compared to the current $28 share price (as of December 10th, 2025), that equates to a 19% discount to fair value.

If VICI returns to fair value and lives up to growth expectations, it could have a 38% upside ahead through 2026. By the end of 2030, the REIT could return 12% annually.

A 6%+ Payout With Room To Grow

VICI's 6.5% forward dividend yield registers comfortably above the real estate sector's 4.9% median forward yield. The REIT's slight selloff in 2025, coupled with its recent dividend hike (more on that in a moment), has widened the forward yield spread by 20 basis points since I last covered it. This is enough for Seeking Alpha's Quant System to award B+ and A- grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively (the latter of which was up from the previous B+ grade).

VICI's immense immediate income is also backed up by its AFFO generation. The company is poised to record an AFFO payout ratio in the mid-70% range for 2025. That's right around the 75% payout ratio that it targets, and, according to the Zen Research Terminal, is superior to the 90% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry.

This is why the Zen Research Terminal anticipates a 1.3% risk of a dividend reduction in the next 12 months. In the next average recession, the probability of a payout cut rises to 4.5%. That's what accounts for the B+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety (up from the prior B grade).

Not surprisingly, I believe that VICI is set up to extend its seven-year dividend growth streak. As it is, this is more established than the sector median of 1.4 years. This explains the Quant System's B grade for overall dividend consistency (down from the previous A- grade).

Following a 4% raise in its quarterly dividend per share to $0.45 in September, the Quant System thinks similar dividend growth is likely from VICI. The Quant System forecasts 4.3% annual forward dividend per share growth from the REIT. That would be over 200 basis points better than the sector median forecast of 2.2%. This warrants a B grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth (down from the previous A grade).

Risks To Consider

VICI is a qualitative REIT. However, the company still has risks that could meaningfully change the investment thesis.

One key concern is Las Vegas visitor volume thus far in 2025. The latest data shows that the city's visitor volume through the first 10 months of the year was 32.3 million people. That's down 7.6% or 2.6 million people from 2024.

Historically, Las Vegas has experienced downturns in tourism in tandem with economic downturns or recessions. At face value, I think that's worrisome that visitor volume is down in a non-recessionary environment. However, I tend to agree with the take from local economist Mike PeQueen.

Data suggests that Las Vegas is emerging into a higher-end market, with it becoming the destination for sports entertainment as of late. This is evidenced by the Super Bowl, the Grand Prix, and WWE WrestleMania coming to town in recent years. As a result, the latest data from the Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority Research Center shows Clark County gross gaming revenue to be up 1%.

From this perspective, it looks like Las Vegas will be fine. Of course, it's worth watching gaming revenue for Clark County moving forward and visitor numbers. That's because about 48% of VICI's total rent revenue is derived from Las Vegas.

Aside from geographic concentration, the other key risk to the REIT is its tenant concentration. As of September 30th, 2025, Caesars Entertainment and MGM Resorts made up 74% of its total annualized cash rent (39% and 35%, respectively). If these tenants encounter any financial issues, VICI could have difficulty collecting rent for the first time since its inception in a worst-case scenario. That could pressure cash flows, resulting in a dividend cut.

Summary: I'm Adding More To This Core Holding

VICI has catalysts to keep growing for years to come. There's the organic growth that's made possible by annual lease escalators. Then, there's the fact that VICI retains plenty of AFFO for selective investments and accompanying business growth. The company's balance sheet is stable as well.

These solid fundamentals also look to come at a dirt-cheap valuation. Shares are deeply undervalued, with nearly 40% upside potential by the end of 2026 and strong return potential through 2030. Earlier this month, I marginally added to my position in VICI. This makes it my 21st-biggest holding, comprising 1.5% of my portfolio. In a few weeks, I anticipate upping my position by another 20% to 25%. That's why I'm sticking with my Buy rating.

data.title: EQT Corporation: Natural Gas Prices Unchained
data.description:
Introduction

The start of the supply chain, or picks and shovels of the AI and data center buildout, is natural gas, which is powering electricity generation (nuclear and uranium are a bit further out, 2030). I have covered key IPPs (independent power producers), the turbine makers, and a host of core data center suppliers, such as semiconductor storage, servers, Ethernet, cooling, and cable suppliers. In August, I dove into the US natural gas sector and came away with two buys, EQT Corporation (EQT) and Expand (EXE), formerly Chesapeake Energy.

Natural Gas Demand

The demand for natural gas is being driven by electricity generation (gas turbines) to feed data centers, warmer summers, and colder winters, as well as LNG exports. The combination, according to Bloomberg, Wood Mackenzie, and JPMorgan, could lead to a tight supply/demand balance that pushes natural gas prices upwards towards the global levels.

The US has benefited from an abundant production of cheap natural gas, with the benchmark prices (HH, or Henry Hub) hovering in the US$2/BTU range for years. The producers developed highly efficient operations and extensive pipeline networks to get the gas to market and saturate consumers, i.e., we are dependent on this fuel source. The rise in LNG, which freezes and compresses the gas for long-distance transport, began to liberate production for far higher-priced export markets. This trend alone would have led to a breakout from the HH US$2/BTU levels. The electricity demand from AI data centers compounds the demand side and should drive prices higher and faster.

Nuclear-powered electricity is rising with the start-up of various dormant plants, but a significant contribution is not likely until beyond 2030, led by the SMR (small modular reactors) technology. While sun and wind have been curtailed by the current administration, they exacerbate the imbalances.

The EQT model

My EQT estimates assume that natural gas prices per cubic foot average US$3.8 in 2025 or about 25% above 2025. This takes into account EQT's hedging strategies and a tight market where demand outpaces supply and storage. A harsher winter or steaming summer can add further upside. For 2027 and 2028 I assume a 5% annual increase. The larger price gains, into the US$8 level, may occur closer to 2030 when the data center and LNG exports result in structural natural gas deficits.

On the cost side, I have a more conservative estimate with the average cost per cubic foot increasing 1% per year vs. material reduction on scale gains, M&A synergies. This results in EBITDA forecasts that are in line with consensus for 2026 and begin to diverge in 2027 and become larger in 2028.

Note that the LNG trading deal should begin in 2030 when the various plants come online. The MVP pipeline is slated to start operation in mid-2028 and is also not included in the estimates.

Financial Estimates

The higher natural gas prices with flattish capex could lead to increasing FCF of over US$4bn, which should result in debt reduction and higher share buybacks. My preferred growth metric is cash earnings (net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation), may grow over 20% on average in the 2026-2028 periods. EQT will probably acquire more production assets and distribution as gas prices climb and deficits intensify.

Peer Comps

I gathered consensus data on producers with at least 30% of revenue derived from natural gas. EQT's consensus estimates are valued at 6.3x P/CE (cash earnings), in line with the peers, but one of the cheapest on a relative or PEG ratio at 0.36x. As with the oil sector, valuations have been de-rated given the weak commodity pricing and seasonal volatility. I would argue that this view should change due to a tighter market and increasing prices. LNG exports are key to leveling US natural gas prices with the rest of the world.

Valuation

I am raising my price target to US$73 from US$61 due to higher estimates and an increase in the target multiple to 7x vs 6x. As discussed in the peer comp section, I believe this sector and especially EQT should garner higher valuations as natural gas prices increase and supply deficits become more normal. While seasonal volatility will persist, data center lead electricity needs and LNG exports, plus greater storage capacity, may reduce price volatility and improve investor confidence.

Risk

While EQT is not a direct AI story, the gas to run the turbines to make the electricity that the data center needs could face issues, and about 30% of new gas demand may not materialize, leading to a drop in prices. The LNG export prices may suffer in an overcapacity situation as other parts of the world build LNG plants, such as in Argentina. Finally, EQT and the sector may face regulatory and environmental headwinds with new administrations that favor renewable energy.

Conclusion

I rate EQT a buy. US natural gas has entered a period of structural deficits with the ability to be exported via LNG, while generation demand for data centers adds further to the demand picture. EQT could see sustained 20% earnings growth as natural gas prices climb toward global levels.

data.title: Why The Next 12-18 Months Are Crucial For IREN's Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The present article explains why the next 12–18 months are crucial for IREN Limited’s (IREN) performance. In my view, IREN’s stock is suitable only for investors with a high risk tolerance, given its high-risk, high-reward profile.

My long-term view on the company is bullish. The paradox is that the market already values the company as an AI infrastructure provider, while most of its current revenue is still generated from its mining operations, does that right? In short yes.

Therefore, IREN’s valuation currently is somewhere between a mining and an AI-related company. I believe investors should view IREN strictly as an AI player—not only because of its deal with Microsoft (MSFT), but also due to its moat in low-cost power consumption and its already-available capacity for large-scale AI workload.

IREN Is Mining or AI Player?

The bitcoin mining business accounts for approximately 97% of total revenue, generating $232M in Q1’26. In total, IREN reported $240M in revenue, representing an impressive 355% YoY and 33% QoQ growth.

IREN announced a 5-year contract with MSFT with a total contract value ((TCV)) of $9.7B, and is expected to generate $1.94B in annual recurring revenue (ARR). Therefore, in the next couple of years—as IREN builds and delivers MSFT’s data center—the company will generate increasingly more revenue from its AI Cloud Services compared to its mining business.

If we annualize the current $240M of revenue, we reach roughly $1B in revenue for FY26. But IREN anticipates $1.9B in ARR from the Microsoft deal—indicating potential revenue growth of at least 90% in the following years. the company is transitioning from a mining business to an AI player, and investors should no longer evaluate it as a mining company despite the (still) low contribution of AI cloud revenue today.

Therefore, in my view, investors should value the company as an AI infrastructure company — keep this point in mind, as we will need it when assessing its valuation.

The Following 12-18 Months Are Crucial

I believe that the following mid-term window is the most important for both the company and its stock performance. Before explaining why, it’s important to note that the company has secured 3GW of data center capacity across multiple sites, while only 460MW—or just 16%—is currently in use. This highlights the company’s huge expansion potential.

Also, based on the company’s statement:

So as I alluded to earlier, a key driver of IREN's competitive advantage in AI cloud services is our vertical integration. We develop our own greenfield sites, engineer our own high-voltage infrastructure, build and operate our own data centers and deploy our own GPUs. Simply put, we control the entire stack from the substation all the way down to the GPU…This model of vertical integration eliminates dependence on third-party colocation providers and most importantly, removes all counterparty risk associated.

Its moat relies on this vertically integrated model, something that MSFT itself highlighted, and in my view was one of the key reasons behind this deal.

Now let me explain the factors behind why I believe the following months are crucial.

  1. First and most obvious is that IREN has to deliver in time the deployment phases related to the Microsoft deal through 2026. Although, the Co-Founder’s statement:

…we have never ever missed a construction or commissioning date in our life as a listed company” is a positive signal, this deal is the first really big and point in the company among AI infrastructure players -like Nebius (NBIS). In my view, if achieve to “meet the contract” will be a significant milestone which easy could attract new customers and hyperscalers.

2. Secondly, in combination with the first factor, the company currently uses only 16% of its total power capacity. IREN has already invested heavily to build or transition its mining operations into AI infrastructure sites. As shown in the table below, IREN’s CapEx is much higher than its operating cash flow (OCF), resulting in the company being a cash-burning business — meaning it simply generates negative free cash flow (FCF).

Notably, IREN plans to invest roughly $5.8B in CapEx for the Microsoft project, and to develop the previously mentioned 3GW capacity sites. The company aims to reach 140,000 GPUs by the end of 2026, compared to 23,000 GPUs today. Microsoft’s 20% prepayment support IREN’s cash position and reduces the need for external financing, but it cannot fully cover the company’s total CapEx needs.

As mentioned:

Looking ahead, we're executing now on a plan that will see our GPU fleet scale from 23,000 GPUs today up to 140,000 GPUs by the end of 2026. When fully deployed, this expansion is expected to support in the order of $3.4 billion in annualized run rate revenue. Importantly, this expansion leverages just 16% of our 3 gigawatts in secured power, leaving ample capacity for future expansion.

It’s obvious that if the company achieve to secure contracts for its full 3GW capacity, then the projected $3.4B in ARR is a realistic target. My risk concern is that the company first develops its AI infrastructure and then signing client deals. To give a complete view of IREN’s future prospects, I believe that we should anticipate additional large contracts with different hyperscalers. I conclude the above after this based on management’s statement:

There is appetite from a number of parties in discussing cloud and other structures well above the 200 megawatts that’s been signed with Microsoft.

3. Reaching my final crucial factor, I want to highlight that the company holds a favorable electricity cost model. According to the company, the cost of electricity to operate its data centers is approximately $0.05/kWh, which is significantly below the industry average of $0.10/kWh.

The vertical integration, as mentioned earlier, and the low cost of electricity are, in my opinion, the company’s main moat catalysts. Investors should monitor whether these catalysts attract new customers and, most importantly, how efficiently the company take advantage of them.

My point, especially regarding electricity costs, is that they are not guaranteed that will stable forever. Currently, IREN offers an extremely low energy cost for its data centers, far below the industry average. Therefore, if the company cannot attract new customers within a reasonable time frame, this will be a negative future catalyst.

Bottom Line

I believe that IREN has huge potential but needs to demonstrate its capabilities to execute the step up (starting with the MSFT deal). My earlier concern remains: the high CapEx requirement combined with the fact that only 16% of its total capacity is currently contracted.

IREN has strong growth catalysts in its vertical integration and low energy costs, and it has already secured 3GW of capacity, signaling that it could become a major AI player. Investors should assess IREN as an AI infrastructure company rather than a mining company.

Moreover, IREN was unprofitable in Q1’26 in terms of operating income. I choose operating income as my key valuation metric because IREN holds financial instruments (specifically Capped Calls) that benefit its reported profitability, which may not fully reflect the company’s real operational performance.

Nowadays, IREN has a market cap of $13B, while its total revenue is roughly $1B annualized, as mentioned earlier. The Price/Operating Income (P/OI) is negative, reflecting an unprofitable company, while the Price/Sales ratio of 15.81 is far above the sector median of 3.55.

Stock seems overvalued and already priced with the MSFT's deal news. However, in my view, the current stock price does not fully reflect the company’s potential. The three crucial factors discussed above, along with its risks, contribute to its high-risk, high-reward profile.

Overall, I rate this stock as BUY, but I suggest it only for investors with high risk tolerance and only as a small portion of their portfolios. I believe that the company’s ability to “meet the MSFT contract,” combined with vertical integration and low energy costs, positions it well to attract new customers.

Therefore, if you are a long-term investor with durability for risk, IREN’s stock is a BUY.

data.title: Real Income Continues To Rise
data.description:
Real Personal Income continues to rise as Light Weight Vehicle SAAR is reduced.

Light Weight Vehicle sales continue sluggish primarily due to Toyota’s (TM) keeping dealer inventories low. Toyota remains the #2 brand in the US market, and the company continues to manufacture vehicles at a pace that is well below demand. Toyota recently had all top 10 slots on the list of fewest days in inventory, an average in the low 30 days of inventory range, while other brands’ vehicles have ranged as high as sitting on lots more than 1yr.

Buyers have been both brand-loyal and sensitive to price as many dealerships loaded vehicles with high-cost options, pushing vehicle costs out of range of many. Toyota, reportedly, has not done that.

There are no signs of pending recession in this data.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Novo Nordisk Is Not Cheap [Rating Downgrade]
data.description:
I bought some Novo Nordisk (NVO)(NONOF) shares earlier this year, and this position is currently down by a double-digit percentage. The continued share price weakness does not look like a purely temporary story at the moment, as long-term earnings projections from Wall Street really declined over the last three months, while the main rival, Eli Lilly (LLY), is in a league of its own. The cautiousness from prominent analysts looks fair as the company continues struggling with revenue growth deceleration while margins stagnate. Moreover, my DCF analysis demonstrates that only extremely aggressive growth assumptions justify NVO's current $200+ billion market cap. I am not selling my NVO shares because there are positives as well. However, I am not buying more shares and might consider resuming buying later, as the share price weakness might persist over the next few months. All in all, I am downgrading NVO to a "Hold".

NVO continues losing ground to LLY

NVO released its latest quarterly earnings on November 5, delivering a solid bottom-line surprise. On the other hand, there was a $323 million revenue miss. It was a second consecutive quarter with no dual beat against consensus estimates, a catalyst that usually undermines investors' confidence.

NVO's revenue grew by 12.4% YoY in Q3, which might seem solid without context. However, any figure becomes much more useful if additional context is added. And the picture looks much worse if we add context. First, there was a rapid revenue growth pace deceleration in Q3 compared to Q2 2025, when NVO recorded a much more aggressive 20.5% YoY growth. Another warning sign is that there was a sequential revenue decline between Q2 and Q3 this year, whereas revenue consistently grew between these quarters in 2021-2024. The fact that the Q2-Q3 revenue seasonality is broken indicates that NVO faces structural headwinds. NVO's main competitor in the GLP-1 market, Eli Lilly, demonstrated accelerated revenue growth in Q3 compared to Q2 [from 38% to 54% YoY increase] and the competitor also demonstrated an aggressive 13% sequential revenue growth in Q3. The fact that LLY demonstrated much stronger topline dynamics over the same period means that the GLP-1 market remained strong, and NVO's weakening revenue pace is explained by the company's weaknesses but not demand softness.

And it is not only about past performance but about future growth potential as well. Thanks to Seeking Alpha's peers functionality, I can easily compare NVO's and LLY's EPS revisions over the last several weeks. As we can see from the comparative table, Eli Lilly runs the show here with net 13 [14 minus 1] upward EPS revisions and net 15 [15 minus 0] upward revenue revisions. Meanwhile, NVO had only down revisions both in terms of EPS and revenue, 3 and 18, respectively.

Finally, NVO's profitability metrics are stagnating. As I mentioned earlier, the company's revenue continued demonstrating double-digit growth over previous quarters. However, solid revenue growth did not result in the expansion of profitability ratios. The fact that NVO's TTM operating margin has declined from 45%+ to around 42% indicates that the company's pricing power is deteriorating because of LLY's success.

With all these structural warning signs, I think that buying more NVO shares does not look like a good investment idea. Moreover, there are questions about NVO's valuation attractiveness.

NVO isn't cheap

Novo Nordisk's valuation ratios are significantly lower than the company's historical averages. However, it is vital to keep in mind that high historical multiples are explained by the company's soaring revenues over several quarters in 2024 and 2023, accompanied by expanding margins. Since NVO's revenue growth is poised to decelerate significantly and margins are likely to stagnate, the deep discount compared to historical averages looks fair. Moreover, the comparison to the sector median looks mixed, and I don't see that it clearly demonstrates undervaluation.

In fact, simulating a discounted cash flow [DCF] model revealed that NVO isn't actually cheap. The DCF's discount rate is 9.43%, which is NVO's cost of equity calculated using the CAPM approach. Cost of equity was selected as a discount rate because of NVO's low leverage.

The company's current market cap is $206 billion, and the TTM levered free cash flow is $5.4 billion. Wall Street analysts expect NVO to demonstrate a modest adjusted EPS growth in Q4 and a mid-single-digit decline in FY2026. However, the TTM cash flow contains a net CAPEX outlier of around $9.1 billion, which is an anomaly compared to previous fiscal years, which is a one-off factor that significantly depressed the TTM-basis FCF.

Therefore, for the base-year FCF assumption, I think that it will be reasonable to add back at least $2.5 billion to the $5.4 billion TTM FCF to make the net CAPEX close to FY2024 levels. I have figured out the $2.5 billion CAPEX adjustment amount by deducting FY2024 net CAPEX of $6.6 billion from the TTM net CAPEX of $9.1 billion. This adjustment of $2.5 billion aims to present a more accurate FCF assumption to avoid unfair understatement. This is particularly important as my assertion is that NVO isn't cheap.

The above scenario was generated to demonstrate what growth assumptions justify NVO's current $206 billion market cap. As we can see, the total fair value estimate of the business is very close to its current market cap if a 4% perpetual growth rate is applied together with a 12.5% FCF CAGR until FY2032. While these assumptions do not appear to be unrealistic given NVO's success in the past, I consider them extremely aggressive for a few reasons.

First of all, the pharmaceutical industry is quite mature in general, and applying a 4% perpetual growth rate for a company from this field is inherently risky. Second, incorporating a 12.5% FCF CAGR for the next seven years is excessively optimistic compared to the approximately mid-single-digit adjusted EPS CAGR projected by Wall Street analysts until FY2032. Of course, NVO has a robust earnings surprise record, but these surprises were usually measured by single-digit percentages. Therefore, expecting that NVO's actual FCF growth will outpace EPS growth forecasts by almost two times is speculative.

With that being said, I think that more realistic growth assumptions should be incorporated. In the second DCF model, I am incorporating a 7% FCF CAGR for the next seven years, which is closer to Wall Street's EPS growth forecasts. Moreover, I am using a slightly more conservative 3.5% perpetual growth rate for the second model. As a result, the business's fair value is $147 billion. I am not suggesting that NVO's market cap will go down that low. However, with the DCF exercise, I wanted to show readers that NVO is unlikely to be cheaply valued.

I tend to agree with the consensus because a 7% growth assumption is quite optimistic compared to the expected ~3% growth of the global GDP by 2030. Moreover, let's not forget that Novo Nordisk represents a very mature pharmaceutical industry, and even taking into account its explosive growth during the early years of GLP-1 accelerated popularity, its FCF CAGR over the past decade was just 2.2%.

On the other hand, incorporating an FCF CAGR closer to mid-single-digits might be unfairly pessimistic because of the GLP-1 exposure and a large pipeline of potential assets. Therefore, a 7% CAGR looks like a golden middle between low double-digit growth and mid-single-digit growth.

Risks to the rating downgrade

NVO's sheer scale is certainly a big advantage because it provides the management with quite a large room to make strategic adjustments. For example, the company employs almost 80 thousand people as of the latest reportable date. Thus, introducing a 10% headcount count means laying off 8,000 employees. If we assume that the annual total cost per employee is $100k, such a layoff might lead to $800 million in yearly savings. Dividing this potential cost saving by around 4.44 billion shares currently outstanding means a $0.18 increase in EPS.

Large scale coupled with NVO's 80%+ gross margin means the company is capable of investing billions in research and development [R&D]. Of course, the entire process of developing a new medicine is quite long and complex, meaning that only a small fraction of assets in the pipeline finally achieve commercialization. However, the more a company is able to invest in R&D, the more diversified its pipeline. Overall, the larger number of potential assets in the pipeline increases the chances of releasing new blockbuster products. And Ozempic's early success demonstrates that sometimes just one new sensational product is enough to drive exponential market cap growth.

Finally, NVO's massive share price drop in 2025 means that its dividend yield increased significantly. The stock currently offers a 3.72% forward dividend yield, which is quite attractive and safe given the company's multi-billion annual FCF even taking into account its aggressive TTM capital investments. Moreover, NVO has a stellar dividend growth record with a 29% CAGR over the last three years. With such a forward yield and historical growth combination, NVO might become increasingly attractive for income investors. An inflow of new investors is likely to trigger a rally, which is a risk to my rating downgrade.

Bottom line

To conclude, NVO is a "Hold". The stock does not look cheap despite low multiples compared to historical averages because its growth prospects are deteriorating. There might be positive surprises and new bold moves from the management that might trigger a rally, which are the main reasons why I prefer to hold and not sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Planet Labs' Q3 2025 Results Show Strength For The Surveillance Industry
data.description:
Thesis

Planet Labs (PL) has just reported some very impressive 3Q25 results, and post-market, the stock traded upwards about 16%. They delivered a 3Q25 revenue of about $81.3 million, a figure that beat estimates by $9.3 million. Non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.00, topping expectations by $0.04. Next quarter, the company is expecting about $76 to $80 million in revenue and an adjusted EBITDA loss somewhere between $5 and $7 million. For FY26, management expects $297 to $301 million in revenue and $6 to $8 million in adjusted EBITDA profit. Both of these guidance figures were well above the consensus of $283.99 million in revenue and FY EPS expectations of –$0.12. In my pre-earnings note, I reinstated my buy call ahead of earnings. I thought we should see some very positive growth with their defence intelligence products being very strong in recurring revenue. Now, heading into 2026, I expect more growth from Planet Labs after they've shown us they're on the right track.

Impressive 3Q25 results

Planet Labs' 3Q25 results were quite impressive, to say the least. Overall, they showed us that the company is heading in the right direction in terms of upping revenue growth and improving cash generation. Revenue hit a record high this quarter at $81.3 million, up 33% year over year, which is the strongest growth from what I can recall in several years and notably faster than prior quarters. We’re seeing demand ramp up for AI-enabled monitoring solutions, defence intelligence contracts, and high-resolution imagery, which is scaling. Very importantly, 97% of the annual contract value was recurring, which goes to show just how sticky and appealing Planet’s subscription-based model is. Now, despite this momentum, GAAP gross margin did slip slightly to 57% from 61%, in my opinion, just a result of a wider mix of data solutions. Operational costs tied to new satellite deployments also increased, and so did amortisation from recent acquisitions. On a non-GAAP basis, gross margin hit 60%, also slightly lower year over year, which might hint at some sustained cost pressure as Planet ramps up the Pelican and other monitoring constellations. Still, operating performance improved quite dramatically, with non-GAAP operating loss narrowing to just $3.4 million, down from the $9.4 million we saw this time last year. Adjusted EBITDA swung to a positive at $5.6 million, which now marks the company’s fourth consecutive EBITDA-positive quarter.

I thought the other impressive feature of the quarter was Planet’s major improvement in cash generation and just all-around balance sheet strength. Year-to-date, the net cash provided by operating activities hit $113.7 million, a hefty turnaround from just $8.1 million last year. Free cash flow for the same period managed to reach $55.2 million, up from $44.9 million this time last year. I take this as an early signal that the business model is maturing nicely and that the scale of recurring revenue is finally starting to outpace capital needs. The company also ended the quarter with $677 million in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments, a figure up 180% year over year. Now, cash was mainly bolstered by a $460 million convertible notes offering we saw earlier. Planet also reported some massive growth in contracted future demand, which was a good sign. The remaining performance obligations jumped 361% year over year to $672 million, and total backlog hit $734 million, about a 216% jump since the same time last year.

Now, these kinds of figures do highlight some very strong forward visibility, mainly driven by wins with the NGA, NRO, NASA, the U.S. Navy, and NATO. Demand with other international defence customers and large commercial partners like AXA is also proving strong. However, even with the strong demand, the company still posted a pretty substantial GAAP net loss of $59.2 million for the quarter, a lot larger than the prior year. This was one negative on earnings, but we can partially put it down to a $43 million negative swing in warrant liability fair value. This was a non-cash and stock-price-dependent item excluded from non-GAAP metrics. So overall, the quarter showed us some very good signs, and it seems the market liked the results, with shares trading upwards almost 16% post-market yesterday.

Defence-related demand

Going into 2026, I’ve been quite bullish on small-cap defence players in general, and after yesterday, Planet Labs is no exception. The biggest booster of their revenue this quarter was strong demand from the government and defence-related contracts, particularly in the defence and intelligence sectors. Now, according to management, this segment saw significant year-over-year growth, which was mainly driven by some big contract wins, such as the $12.8 million NGA Luno B prime award. We also saw the renewed and expanded work with the National Reconnaissance Office, NASA task orders, and even U.S. Navy renewals. NATO expansions and other defence and intelligence renewals were also predominant, altogether making up a large portion of the overall revenue increase. Now, this government/defence demand contributed pretty disproportionately to the 33% year-over-year revenue jump to $81.3 million and was cited as a big driver of better-than-expected results.

Now, in the intelligence space, quite a few companies have what we call a subscription-based model in which a timed contract is formed for access to Planet Labs satellites. The result of this is that Planet Labs’ defence and intelligence products are very sticky because they can deliver continuous, time-sensitive data that customers can’t replace or replicate with one-off imagery. The company has a wide array of satellites appealing to lots of needs, so systems like Pelican high-resolution tasking, SkySat, and PlanetScope daily monitoring become integrated into military planning. The clients build their decision outcomes on this data and put into place routine tasks, protocols, all dependent on the imagery they get. For example, lots of people work on intelligence workflows, meaning the users on the ground depend on fresh, frequently updated imagery. An area could be first monitored using data from the Pelican, and then, if movement is flagged, the users have protocols to send out low-flying drones to further scan the area. So if they want to maintain operational awareness going into next quarter, they won't be changing just the provider of imagery but having to build a completely new workflow. Another example would be the crossover between quarters. If a defence customer used Pelican tasking to track vehicle movements near a very strategic border in the prior quarter, that insight becomes outdated within days. So to maintain the same level of visibility, they have to renew their subscription for ongoing monitoring. This real-time operational dependency is what makes Planet’s defence offerings naturally recurring and difficult to replace, which is why the renewal demand was so high.

The downside: a premium valuation

As you would expect, Planet Labs’ valuation is still pretty rich relative to the fundamentals. The stock is trading at a forward price to sales of about 13.92x against a sector median of just 1.74. Similarly, on a trailing twelve-month basis, its EV to sales ratio sits at 14.10x, also way above the sector’s 2.18x ratio. The market cap now sits at $3.95 billion, with a $3.70 billion enterprise value, and still no meaningful earnings. So the valuation assumes substantial future revenue growth and margin expansion despite this current unprofitability. I also like to compare Planet Labs to a couple of other players who have very similar products in terms of intelligence services based on subscription packages. BlackSky (BKSY) and Spire Global (SPIR) also have surveillance satellites. You can see the valuation comparison below.

However, even comparing Planet Labs against those two players shows just how much of a premium the company carries. Planet Labs has a far more diverse product portfolio and even a more diverse customer base. I also think they're a bit ahead in terms of growth trajectory and are already showing us the ability to lock in recurring customers. For these reasons, the market has bought into the stock.

Looking ahead

I think another good sign for the industry will be when BlackSky reports earnings in early March. If we see general surveillance contracts ramp up, it tells me government spending on satellite intelligence is growing. However, it's no secret now that the demand for this high-cadence imagery/AI-enabled monitoring is stepping up.

data.title: Dino Polska: Real Problem Or Opportunity?
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Dino Polska (DNOPY)(DNOPF) was in June, to analyze its Q1 results. Since then, the stock has not performed as I expected, clearly underperforming the index, even though the results were not bad and it was executing well on its long-term plan.

My goal with today's post is to analyze what has happened during this time and try to explain the more than 30% drop since the historical highs set, precisely, shortly after the publication of my article.

Dino is a curious company. It only reports quarterly and does not do analyst conferences. This can be attractive for those who do not have much time to follow their portfolio companies, especially for the peace of mind that comes from having the founding CEO at the head of the company (51% of outstanding shares), without any media appearances and only focused on executing the expansion and business plan. This little communication can only happen in companies with such a CEO.

Thesis Review

This is a fragment from my previous article that I believe summarizes the company very well.

Let's briefly recall the thesis for Dino Polska. Dino is a Polish supermarket chain with a strong presence in the western part of the country. It's characterized by having a founder-led management, high vertical integration, ownership of its store locations and the land it operates on, as well as its own meat processing plants and distribution centers.

The company's main competitive advantage lies in its "local monopolies" in rural areas of the country. By focusing mainly on towns with no more than 5,000 inhabitants and maintaining a high level of agility in building stores (the company owns the firm that constructs its supermarkets), Dino makes it unprofitable for other competitors to enter these market niches.

If we recall, Dino's long-term bullish thesis is based on its ability to continue expanding the number of stores in Poland (currently 2,688) while maintaining organic growth above inflation. We will see how well this combination holds up, but it should drive sales growth in the mid-single-digit range. With good capital allocation, bottom-line growth should be in the high-double-digit range. Let's review its figures.

Earnings Review

If I say hardly anything about Q2, it is because it is like reading a carbon copy of Q3, in fact, the documents presented by the company are practically identical.

What I would highlight most from Q2, is the explanation for the increase in personnel costs (+22.7% YoY) that appeared in the earnings report:

This growth was a consequence of the higher number of Dino Group employees, from 45,274 as of June 30, 2024 to 53,523 as of June 30, 2025, in connection with the expansion of the size of the Dino Group business, the expansion of the Dino store network, the increase in LFL sales in existing stores and the higher average salary within the Dino Group.

Going back to more recent times, on November 6, Dino presented Q3 earnings.

The first thing I would highlight, one of the pillars of the investment thesis, is that the pace of new store openings has accelerated again (98 vs. 68 in Q3 2024). It already has 2,933 stores in the country (2,774 have their own solar panels, increasing their energy self-sufficiency).

This is good news, especially considering the potential TAM that Dino has, which is estimated to be at least 7,000 stores within Poland. This is undoubtedly one of the company's main growth drivers and the main reason behind its capital reinvestment (more on this later).

It is important to consider that the maximum levels of store maturity and profitability are reached after 4-5 years. So we are probably still a long way from seeing Dino's true returns.

The other driver of sales is organic store growth, the so-called LFL. This growth has to be compared to inflation in order to arrive at the real growth of Dino stores.

Gone are the days of stellar store growth of almost 30%. It is normal; when inflation falls, organic growth decreases, and even more so when it is a low-cost operator.

If inflation is higher than store LFLs, real growth ends up being negative. Unfortunately, this is starting to become more common than in the past. The erratic behaviors of the last two quarters are due to the Easter vacations falling in different quarters in 2023 vs. 2024.

And, frankly, I think this is the metric that is currently moving Dino's share price.

LFLs this year are being chaotic. Two quarters of growth below inflation and one way above due to a change at Easter week from last year. The board doesn't have much communication with the investor. I think it is unclear what a normalized year would have looked like and if all quarters would have been below inflation in the absence of the calendar change.

The fusion of poor communication, with less liquidity than in other countries, is what leads this stock to suffer such large (30%) declines on an ongoing basis. Or at least this is my theory.

What should reassure shareholders is knowing that the founding CEO is behind it and that they are still executing in an accelerated manner this year, so perhaps this will turn out to be a one-off this year and really have little impact on LP's thesis. In the valuation part we'll see....

The combination of these two metrics gives us the company's sales growth, a good 15.17%, although we have already seen that it has been completely driven by the increase in the number of stores.

What has surprised me for the better is the remarkable increase in the EBITDA margin to 8.6%. Anyway, the same quarter last year was 8.9%. It is curious how there is a clear seasonality in this figure, without any really clear explanation. In 2023 history repeats itself.

The reasons for this quarter fall within the normal and expected range:

  • Better cost control (lower growth than sales).
  • Fresh products (such as meat and sausages) accounted for 40.2% of sales in Q3 2025, compared to 38.6% in Q3 2024. These products are more profitable, especially since Dino produces part of them internally (Agro-Rydzyna plant).
  • The new logistics center in Brzeg completed construction in Q3, improving distribution efficiency (although it opens in Q4).

Although at the annual level there are more compelling reasons. The main factor behind this margin compression has been lower product prices and, above all, higher labor costs. This category of spending has experienced a lagged effect, as final price inflation hit the country in 2022 and 2023, but wages did not increase significantly until 2024.

On the other hand, the company's financial position is already almost net cash. We should see savings in Interest Expenses.

Capital Allocation

Here lies one of the most important points of Dino's thesis.

Like any good supermarket, Dino Polska has a Negative Working Capital. This generally makes the OCF higher than the NOPAT.

Historically, Dino has not generated positive FCF, because it reinvested all cash in CAPEX to open stores.

The previous two years have been quite exceptional, as there was positive FCF in both years because, for the first time, they did not invest all the cash in CAPEX. This made me think that maybe Dino could start implementing a policy of returning capital to the shareholder, but we can see that, for the moment, it will have to wait.

It is fine for a company to reinvest all its cash, as long as it has lines of business with interesting ROICs (higher than WACC).

Dino's historical returns (CROICs), as shown in the picture, have been 30% and ROCEs of 15%. If it reinvests 100% of the cash at these rates of return, the future bottom-line growth of the business should not stray far from these growth rates (25.9% CAGR in the last 6 years).

This year they have reaccelerated store openings, reinvestment opportunities have returned, and FCF has fizzled again, but I wouldn't worry; we'll see what metrics are used to value this company later. Dino Polska is expected to close 2025 with between 320 and 330 new openings, rates not seen since 2021-2022.

Anyway, this has an impact on working capital... Increase in inventories: Dino has expanded its store network (+245 new stores in 2025), which implies more initial stock for openings. If last year it had a cash inflow of 345M ZLTs, this year, so far, it has an outflow of 50M ZLTs.

And this is how Dino's capital reinvestment and value creation process works, which is a textbook example of a compounder company.

Valuation

Dino does not buy back shares or distribute dividends, so our yield as shareholders, at least on that side, is 0%.

Moreover, as FCF is negative, we will have to focus on the growth of a metric that replaces FCF, EBITDA. In Dino's case, this is the metric that the market looks at the most.

Estimating Dino's growth is actually quite simple. We have to play with 3 things:

  • Rate of openings
  • LFL growth
  • EBITDA margin

I originally thought the way to do this was to project a percentage on the growth rate of these stores, but if you do that, you end up with a larger number opening each year, which is unlikely in reality.

So I think the best thing to do is to play around with the number of stores we think you can open per year. I think this number will be between 300 and 350. This leaves us with growth rates of between 9.4% and 10.4% on this metric.

Then we have to see what LFL growth rate Dino is capable of. I think it will be closer to 4% than anything else. We have seen before how, as inflation falls in Poland, this number falls as well.

In the lowest scenario, which I will use, it gives us sales growth of 13.9% until 2029.

Now we have to see what EBITDA margin the company may be able to achieve in 2029. If we have lower sales growth, there may be less operating leverage, so I have decided to increase the margin to only 8.5% in 2029. Maybe it will end up being 9%, who knows, but I say I want to make a conservative case.

With this, we get 15.5% growth in EBITDA.

The exit multiple chosen is 15X EBITDA, which, from the 38 ZLTs that I find interesting, gives us an IRR of 17.6% in the most base case of all to 2029.

Moreover, this average double-digit return would be achieved in the most conservative case of all. We could put the opening rate at 350, LFL at 5%, and EBITDA margin at 9%, for a return of 22%. In addition, this could lead to a higher multiple, increasing our shareholder return.

For all these reasons, I rate the stock as a buy.

Conclusion

Dino may be an interesting investment idea away from the AI boom. A company that is easy to understand, with a clear execution plan, and does not require a lot of follow-up work. The thesis is clear: just let the CEO execute.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: STEW: Discounted Valuation Justifies A Buy
data.description:
Overview

While ETFs are usually the most popular approach for investors to get diversity across equities, this may not be the best approach for some investors. An index like the S&P 500 has a lot of companies that investors may not be excited to hold. This is where a more concentrated approach like the SRH Total Return Fund (STEW) comes into play. STEW takes a more focused approach to its portfolio and management selects what it believes to be the highest quality assets based on its bottom-up value driven selection process. When I previously covered STEW, I issued a buy rating due to the tax-efficient nature of the distributions. However, I wanted to revisit the fund's performance and value proposition as we approach the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that STEW's share price has increased by about 7.9%. This demonstrates the fund's ability to participate in the positive momentum of the market rally over the last few months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, STEW's total return jumps up to 11.4% over the same time frame. STEW now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 4.5% while offering attractive dividend growth over a longer holding period. This also makes the fund an attractive choice for investors seeking a source of traditional dividend growth.

Additionally, the fund continues to trade at a deeply discounted price to NAV valuation. This can be an indication that it's an attractive time to accumulate shares on its current weakness. While there are some risks to consider, such as underperformance against peers, the fund is still a great long-term position for investors looking to get the best of both worlds: price growth and a growing stream of dividend income. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that STEW implements.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, STEW has total managed assets of $2.44B that are allocated across 23 different equity positions. Management's strategy is to identify investment opportunities that will provide attractive returns over a long period of time. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 1.14%, but this is a bit misleading because it includes other fees related to operating expenses. When excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits closer to 0.99%.

Despite its focused approach to holdings, the fund has an efficient level of diversity across different industries. As we can see below, STEW is most heavily allocated to Diversified investments at 36.87%, which represents businesses that have exposure to a diverse range of sectors. This is followed by an allocation to Diversified Financial Services and Consumer Services, which accounts for 22.85% and 5.13% of assets respectively. However, the focus on the top two industries presents a degree of concentration risk if the Financial industry experiences headwinds.

When looking closer at the holdings, it makes sense why the fund is so focused on a small industry concentration. Since the top holding is Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), it's clear why STEW's breakdown by industry is most heavily allocated to Diversified investments. BRK.B essentially provides indirect exposure to some of the greatest companies in the world.

Fellow analyst, James Foord, published an article highlighting BRK.B's latest 13F. However, we can see that STEW also provides direct exposure to high quality businesses including JPMorgan (JPM), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Microsoft (MSFT), and Evercore (EVR), just to name a few. Therefore, STEW is an easy way to instantly gain exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world, while trimming the fat of indexes like the S&P 500. However, the top ten holdings make up over 88% of total assets, which does present concentration risk.

Performance Insights & Comparison

However, investors need to understand that STEW's higher dividend yield means that its performance may have some limitations. For instance, we can refer to the long-term performance of the fund below. As we can see, STEW's total return sits at 243% over the last ten years. Despite the fund's higher starting dividend yield, the fund still underperforms a more traditional index-tracking ETF like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) over the same time frame. Since STEW prioritizes income generation, the fund has actively sold off portions of the upside price movement of its holdings. Therefore, the price growth of STEW is ultimately limited compared to traditional growth focused ETFs.

Additionally, there are several closed-end funds that aim to provide attractive total returns through a portfolio of equities. While their underlying selection process for its holdings and breakdown by sector may be different, the underlying objective to provide attractive total returns while providing a high dividend yield remains similar. I believe that the following two funds are the most compelling peers out there:

  • Virtus Total Return Fund (ZTR): 9.5% dividend yield.
  • Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund (CSQ): 6.3% dividend yield.

As we can see below, CSQ is the largest in terms of assets under management at $3.3B. CSQ's standout feature is that it utilizes a blend of both equities and debt securities. However, STEW has the longest operating history that dates back to 1972, which indicates a long track record of success and management's ability to adapt to different market cycles. Conversely, ZTR is the smallest fund in terms of assets under management and also has the shortest operating history with an inception dating back to 2005. ZTR's standout feature is that it takes a global approach to its assets, with exposure to equities that have primarily operations outside of the US.

When running a performance comparison with this peer group, we can see that CSQ is the winner here. Over the last ten years, CSQ has the highest total return of 345.8% when including distributions paid. This means that CSQ significantly outperforms STEW's total return of 243% over the same time frame. However, STEW's underlying strategy demonstrates the largest level of price growth potential of the peer group. STEW's higher price growth can likely be attributed to the fact that the fund simply has the lowest dividend yield of the peer group.

Despite the fund's underperformance against CSQ, a case can still be made for the value proposition of STEW at this time. Since STEW is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, STEW now trades at a large discount to NAV of 21.1%. For reference, STEW has traded at an average discount to NAV of 18.81% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the chart below, we can see that STEW's current discount to NAV sits at the larger end of its ten-year range. This can be an indication that it's a good time to accumulate shares on the weakness.

However, the recent price to NAV discount may be attributed to the fact that Berkshire Hathaway's performance has remained lackluster compared to the indexes. Just as a reference point, we can see how BRK.B has underperformed SPY and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) over the last three-year period.

Dividend Strength

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.20 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 4.5%. This latest payout represents a sizeable 21.2% raise from the prior quarter's distribution of $0.165 per share. When it comes to a reliable source of income generation, STEW is a great pick for investors seeking stability. The fund is an outlier amongst peer closed-end funds because STEW has provided a sense of traditional dividend growth in addition to its high starting dividend yield. For instance, STEW has raised its dividend at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 10.10% over the last five years, which is extremely impressive.

Since the fund has historically demonstrated no issues generating earnings that exceed the distributions, the fund is likely to continue raising dividends over time. This makes STEW a great choice for investors that want to see their annual passive income grow, without the need to consistently add new capital. Additionally, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that the majority of the payouts are issued in a tax-efficient manner, which limits the overall tax burden for investors. This is achieved through the usage of net realized long-term gains and return of capital, which have favorable tax treatment for investors.

Just to demonstrate the strength of the fund's dividend raises, I ran a back test of an original $10,000 investment at the start of 2016. This back test assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial $10K. It also assumes that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of more shares. As we can see below, investors would have been able to increase their annual dividend income by simply remaining invested:

2016 Dividend Income: $527

2025 Dividend Income: $1,221

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on STEW at this time due to the attractive price to NAV valuation. The fund continues to trade at a compelling price to NAV discount, which may be attributed to Berkshire Hathaway's recent weakness. Despite this, I believe that this price disconnect presents an attractive opportunity for long-term investors. The fund still has an excellent history of dividend raises over time, while also prioritizing tax-efficient distributions for shareholders. The fund takes a more focused approach than traditional index-tracking ETFs, which may be an efficient way to trim the fluff of the index.

data.title: PAR Technology: The Reset Is Done, Now Execution Decides The Stock
data.description:
PAR Technology Corporation's (PAR) valuation reset earlier this year is what pulled me back in. The stock now trades near 4x revenue, and Q3 showed enough progress for me to stay constructive. ARR reached about $298 million, up 22% YoY, and subscription revenue grew 25 percent to roughly $74.8 million, so we can see a steadier run rate for the business.

Moving forward, my focus is on how the stock responds if PAR keeps rolling out new sites faster and manages to maintain mid-teens organic ARR (annual recurring revenue) growth. I believe the stock price has dropped steeply over the last few months because the rate of deployment with big customers like Burger King and Popeyes hasn’t been on schedule. That should change over the next quarter or so, and the company is also doing a good job with its attach rates.

I’m still bullish on the company’s fundamentals driving the upside in the near term, even with all the noise.

The Two Things PAR Must Get Right From Here

The real question for me now is how quickly PAR can turn the contracted demand into live sites. ARR looks good on paper, but the stock will only work if implementations move at a steady pace. I kept thinking about the Burger King pause earlier in the year because it showed the entire tension in one moment. The company already had the contract, but it was rather difficult to roll out POS, loyalty, and data together. However, we’ve had constant messaging from management that it has been working through those issues all year, and the tone across the last two earnings calls suggests onboarding is in a better place heading into 2026.

Following on from that, the speed at which PAR can implement at its sites is crucial because the economics per site are now stronger than before. As I mentioned in the introduction, Q3 ARR came in at about $298 million, up 22%, and the quality in the wins is evident with the attach rates. Every new POS customer in the quarter took at least one other product, usually loyalty or data, and payments attach continued to improve. That tells me customers are leaning into the platform, not treating Brink as a stand-alone system.

We must also consider how durable its ARR is. You cannot reach mid-teens organic growth without stable renewals and deeper relationships with your existing customers, and PAR is executing well on that front. Its subscription revenue reached roughly $74.8 million, and total revenue grew 23% YoY to $119.2 million.

To be clear, the company still needs to handle concentration risk. RBI is its biggest customer, so anything that happens with that relationship affects the narrative far more than any other group. I like that the partnership is expanding, but the downside is partly what we’ve seen this year with the swings in rollouts.

Finally, it has improved its general financial and operational metrics. EBITDA and cash flow are up, so the inference is that we’re seeing the heavy investment cycle slowly ending. All things considered, Q3 has reinforced my confidence in PAR’s trajectory.

Q3 Snapshot

Metric

Q3 2025

YoY Change

Total revenue

$119.2 million

+23%

Subscription revenue

$74.8 million

+25%

Total ARR

$298 million

+22%

Subscription gross margin

~55%

—

Adjusted EBITDA

$5.8 million

+$3.4 million

Operating cash flow

$8.4 million

Strong improvement

GAAP net loss

$18.2 million

Narrowed

Why the Price Gives Me Room, and What Could Still Test My Patience

The reset is the major reason I am even more comfortable staying constructive here. PAR now trades around 4x revenue, which is a very different setup from the 7.5x range that made me call a Hold back in June. It is still fairly close to the multiples we saw in September, when I upgraded my rating to a buy. At this level, the stock still carries all the baggage from the operational noise that has been present all year, without accounting for the company’s medium- and long-term prospects.

Company Comparison Table

Company

Price/Sales

EV/Sales

How Peers Compare

PAR

~3.2x

~4x

Reset multiple. Improving cash flow.

Toast

~3.4x

~3x

Has a larger base.

NCR Voyix

~0.5x

~1x

Very mature profile. Not comparable on unit economics.

In my opinion, PAR deserves to sit above NCR on both multiples above because its recurring mix and the unit economics are fundamentally different. Sure, they serve similar markets but in different areas, and NCR is a much bigger company. With Toast, it’s a bit harder to justify a premium based on their forward growth rates, but PAR has better gross margins. And if it can keep its deployments on schedule and maintain consistent ARR growth, then we should see a rerating over the near term.

Having said that, we still need to keep an eye on a few things. I already touched on the customer concentration risk and rollout execution, and they affect PAR’s margin development. Yes, its subscription service margin is great (25% higher YoY), but hardware is still weighing on the consolidated margins. The company needs to keep improving its operating leverage via software and payments so that the overall figure goes up.

Besides that, the company trades in a segment where sentiment can shift pretty quickly. We might see the stock pull back again if SaaS multiples across the board start to compress, so it’s not a company-specific flaw, but I thought to point it out.

Then, TASK. Management is deferring TASK rollout and revenue to 2026 so it can chase the bigger international chains. I think the tradeoff makes sense strategically, but it also comes with timing risks. As investors, it’s normal to want to see the first meaningful Tier 1 deployment before we assign a value to that pipeline. So, we’re left waiting on TASK as a 2026 story, not a near-term growth story.

If PAR Executes, The Stock Will Rerate

I see PAR in a far healthier position now than it was at the beginning of this year, even though the valuation is more reflective of the noise around the company at several points over the last few quarters. Generally speaking, the company is doing better in terms of its operations, and I don’t think profitability is too far off. Given all these factors, I’m confident that the stock will rise again once we see better execution heading into 2026.

data.title: IIF: Historical Outperformance And Superior Industry Approach
data.description:
MS India Investment Fund Outlook

Indian equities have been on a tear in the past decade, and while the economy and stock market still have a favorable outlook ahead of them, many investors are concerned about valuations and economic risks this year.

As a result, Indian equities have lost steam this year and have had flat performance, while other Asian stock markets like South Korea have been some of the main drivers of the favorable performance of emerging markets.

India still has a lot of appeal going for it. Economic growth will outpace that of many emerging market peers, and the Indian consumer will be a strong driver of the country's growth, while export performance may continue to gyrate.

The Morgan Stanley India Investment Fund (IIF) looks like a good vehicle to accumulate on any potential dips in the coming months, as this would result in an even deeper discount to NAV. This fund invests in some of the leading companies in India, including favorable-performing industries like banking and consumer-themed industries.

MS India Investment Fund Overview

The MS India Investment Fund is a closed-end fund that utilizes a top-down and bottom-up approach to invest in equities in India. It invests in many of the dominant large-cap companies in indices such as the MSCI India Index.

IIF is a relatively smaller fund with around $280 million in assets, which has a 1.31% net expense ratio.

One appeal of this closed-end fund is that it trades at a 10% discount to NAV, which is slightly more than its historical average. IIF is a great fund to take advantage of during pullbacks in the markets, as this discount can often widen.

The MS India Investment Fund has been a clear victor in terms of relative performance, trouncing the MSCI India Index since its inception in the 1990s.

The Morgan Stanley India Investment Fund has also outperformed other India ETFs, including those that focus on small-caps as well as other ETFs that track the MSCI India Index.

Moving forward, IIF looks like one of the most favorable India funds out there, but it may be more vulnerable to sharper drawdowns during any market corrections, as many closed-end funds are.

One of the strong appeals of this fund is its massive exposure to financial and consumer companies, which collectively account for around 70% of its total assets.

The Indian consumer is one of the strongest consumers in Asia, and areas like banking have witnessed substantial growth due to rising penetration rates.

  • Loan growth in India has been over 11% between 2012 and 2025. This high-growth environment will continue to support banks.
  • India's urban population is growing very rapidly and is projected to account for 40% of the population by 2030
  • According to India's Retail Association, retail sales recently grew by 5% and are projected to pick up to 9-10%.

IIF’s top 10 holdings account for around 50% of the fund’s assets. It invests in some of the country's leading banks, as well as other dominant consumer companies.

One appeal of this strategy is that you can invest in some of India’s leading large-cap companies, which major indices track, at a 10+% discount to NAV. The MSCI India Index invests in many of these same companies.

However, this index has a lower weighting in financials and consumer companies, as these industries only account for around 48% of the index.

India's Economic Outlook

India's economy has been experiencing favorable growth despite trade war tensions, which have hit its exports. Consumer growth will continue to support the economy, and bank credit growth remains favorable. With inflation under control, India's central bank is also in a better position to cut rates.

Stronger Recent Growth: India’s economy had one of its strongest quarters recently despite concerns over tariffs. Despite the impact of partial 50% tariffs from the United States, India’s economy still grew by 8.2% last quarter. This is a massive improvement from Q3 2024, when growth was only 5.4%.

India Quarterly GDP Growth Reaches New High

Steady PMI: Manufacturing growth in India has continued to remain very healthy, with PMI readings near 60 in recent months. India’s manufacturing PMI and IIP growth have both remained very favorable. Massive growth in FDI in manufacturing in the past decade has been a massive driver of the industry's growth.

Inflation is Under Control: Inflation has been on a massive downtrend since it peaked in 2021 and 2022. The lower GST for food and other measures have helped ease inflation and improve consumer sentiment. Food inflation, including in rural areas, has also been on a massive downtrend since 2024.

Debt Is Poised to Improve: One major concern rising among emerging markets is the rising sovereign debt levels, which are projected to continue increasing this decade in many emerging markets. Although its debt levels are considerably high, India’s public debt is poised to improve in the coming years, eventually allowing it to fall below the emerging market average.

Consumer Sentiment: The consumer market appears very healthy and will continue to be a major driver of growth in the coming years, helping to offset weaker export growth. This should bode well for ETFs like this that are focused on financial and consumer companies.

Peer ETFs and Final Thoughts

The Morgan Stanley India Investment Fund has outperformed peer ETFs that track the MSCI India Index and has also outperformed Indian small caps. However, the dominant driver behind this rally in Indian equities, which this fund partially captures, has been the rapid growth of Indian consumer stocks.

The Columbia India Consumer ETF (INCO) outperformed this ETF by over 40 percentage points during this 12+ year period.

The biggest concern with Indian equity markets now is their massive premium to emerging markets. In this environment, it is best to ensure that you are accessing some of the high-growth consumer themes to justify the premium to other emerging Asian markets.

MSCI India

The Morgan Stanley India Investment Fund looks like a better bet than other funds that track the MSCI India Index, especially since it has a higher weighting in consumer stocks and has a historical benchmark of outperforming. In this environment, it is best to accumulate this fund on any pullbacks as its discount to NAV potentially widens.

India's economic outlook still appears very strong, despite the export shocks of this year, and equities look like a reasonable hold, with the main risk being a mean reversion in valuation.
